request.question: This U.S. state's oldest school, Berwick Academy, overlooks the Salmon Falls River which forms part of the state border with New Hampshire. Name the state.

title: Newichawannock Canal
Vaughan Woods State Park
Portland & Rochester Railroad
Salmon Falls River
Great East Lake
Lovell Lake
Berwick Academy (Maine)
Salmon Falls Mill Historic District
Little River (Salmon Falls River)
Horn Pond
sentences: The Newichawannock Canal is a man-made canal which drains Great East Lake into Horn Pond at the border between Wakefield, New Hampshire, and Acton, Maine, in the northeastern United States.
It is at the head of the Salmon Falls River, which the Abenaki called "Newichawannock", meaning "river with many falls".

Begun in 1850 by the Great Falls Company to increase the water available for its mills in Somersworth, New Hampshire, the 0.75 mi canal is unusual as it was built for strictly industrial purposes in an area remote from the actual industrial site.
The canal forms the boundary between the states of New Hampshire and Maine, and is spanned by a stone bridge built at the same time.
The canal and bridge, along with related artifacts, were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2014.
Vaughan Woods State Park is a public recreation area located along the Salmon Falls River on the western edge of South Berwick, Maine, at the New Hampshire border.
The state park includes the restored Hamilton House, stands of old-growth forest, and 3 mi of hiking trails.
The lands were bequeathed to the state by Elizabeth Vaughan in 1949.
The Portland and Rochester Railroad, established in 1867, was an important predecessor railway of the Rochester to Portland branch line of the Boston and Maine Railroad.

It was founded in the merger of several smaller shortline rail transport companies, the oldest being the shortline York and Cumberland Railroad which was formed in 1846 to connect the seaport facilities of Portland, Maine, to the water powered manufacturing and textile industries along the Quampheagan Falls on the Salmon Falls River in the twin towns of South Berwick, Maine, and Rollinsford, New Hampshire (ca 1850).
The Salmon Falls River is a tributary of the Piscataqua River in the U.S. states of Maine and New Hampshire.

It rises at Great East Lake, Newichawannock Canal, and Horn Pond and flows south-southeast for approximately 38 mi , forming the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire.
Great East Lake is an 1825 acre water body located in Carroll County, New Hampshire, and York County, Maine, in the United States.
The lake is shared by the towns of Wakefield, New Hampshire, and Acton, Maine, with 45% of its surface area in Acton and 55% in Wakefield.
The lake is the farthest upstream of the five headwater lakes of the Salmon Falls River, a tributary of the Piscataqua River.
Water from Great East Lake flows out its dam and through the 800 ft Newichawannock Canal into Horn Pond, then into the Salmon Falls River below Horn Pond.
Lovell Lake is a 538 acre water body in Carroll County in eastern New Hampshire, United States, in the town of Wakefield.
The lake is both spring-fed and fed by small streams including Horse Brook.
The outlet of the lake, located at the village of Sanbornville, is the beginning of the Branch River.
From here, the Branch River flows through Milton where it joins the Salmon Falls River on the Maine-New Hampshire border.
The Salmon Falls River empties into the tidal waters of the Piscataqua River northwest of Portsmouth, New Hampshire, then into the Gulf of Maine.
Berwick Academy is a college preparatory school located in South Berwick, Maine.
Founded in 1791, it is the oldest educational institution in Maine and one of the oldest private schools in North America.
The school sits on a 72-acre, 11-building campus on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire.

Approximately 565 students in grades Pre-K through 12 (and Post-Grad) attend this coeducational day school, from approximately 60 communities in the surrounding regions of southern Maine, southeastern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.
The Salmon Falls Mill Historic District encompasses a historic mill complex on Front Street in Rollinsford, New Hampshire.
The complex includes four major structures and seven smaller ones, on about 14 acre of land along the Salmon Falls River.

They were built between about 1840 and the mid-1860s, and have an unusual architectural unity, for additions made to the buildings were done with attention to matching elements of the existing structures.
The Number 2 Mill, built in 1848, was an early location where a turbine was used instead of a waterwheel to provide power to the mill machinery.
The district was listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 1980.
The Little River is a 21.3 mi tributary of the Salmon Falls River in the U.S. state of Maine.
It rises from streams in York County, flows southwest through Lebanon, and reaches its confluence with the Salmon Falls River in Berwick.
Horn Pond is a 227 acre water body located on the border of Acton, Maine, and Wakefield, New Hampshire, in the United States.
The lake is both spring-fed and fed by the outflows of Great East Lake to the north and Wilson Lake to the east.

Horn Pond's outlet is the Salmon Falls River which forms a natural border between Maine and New Hampshire and empties into the Piscataqua River east of Dover, New Hampshire, and ultimately the Gulf of Maine.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: Berwick Academy (Maine) | Berwick Academy is a college preparatory school located in South Berwick, Maine.  Founded in 1791, it is the oldest educational institution in Maine and one of the oldest private schools in North America.  The school sits on a 72-acre, 11-building campus on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire.  Approximately 565 students in grades Pre-K through 12 (and Post-Grad) attend this coeducational day school, from approximately 60 communities in the surrounding regions of southern Maine, southeastern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.

Salmon Falls River | The Salmon Falls River is a tributary of the Piscataqua River in the U.S. states of Maine and New Hampshire.  It rises at Great East Lake, Newichawannock Canal, and Horn Pond and flows south-southeast for approximately 38 mi , forming the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire.

Vaughan Woods State Park | Vaughan Woods State Park is a public recreation area located along the Salmon Falls River on the western edge of South Berwick, Maine, at the New Hampshire border.  The state park includes the restored Hamilton House, stands of old-growth forest, and 3 mi of hiking trails.  The lands were bequeathed to the state by Elizabeth Vaughan in 1949.

Horn Pond | Horn Pond is a 227 acre water body located on the border of Acton, Maine, and Wakefield, New Hampshire, in the United States.  The lake is both spring-fed and fed by the outflows of Great East Lake to the north and Wilson Lake to the east.  Horn Pond's outlet is the Salmon Falls River which forms a natural border between Maine and New Hampshire and empties into the Piscataqua River east of Dover, New Hampshire, and ultimately the Gulf of Maine.

Newichawannock Canal | The Newichawannock Canal is a man-made canal which drains Great East Lake into Horn Pond at the border between Wakefield, New Hampshire, and Acton, Maine, in the northeastern United States.  It is at the head of the Salmon Falls River, which the Abenaki called "Newichawannock", meaning "river with many falls".  Begun in 1850 by the Great Falls Company to increase the water available for its mills in Somersworth, New Hampshire, the 0.75 mi canal is unusual as it was built for strictly industrial purposes in an area remote from the actual industrial site.  The canal forms the boundary between the states of New Hampshire and Maine, and is spanned by a stone bridge built at the same time.  The canal and bridge, along with related artifacts, were listed on the National Register of Historic Places in 2014.
Berwick, Maine | Berwick is a town in York County, Maine, United States, situated in the southern part of the state beside the Salmon Falls River.

Lovell Lake | Lovell Lake is a 538 acre water body in Carroll County in eastern New Hampshire, United States, in the town of Wakefield.  The lake is both spring-fed and fed by small streams including Horse Brook.  The outlet of the lake, located at the village of Sanbornville, is the beginning of the Branch River.  From here, the Branch River flows through Milton where it joins the Salmon Falls River on the Maine-New Hampshire border.  The Salmon Falls River empties into the tidal waters of the Piscataqua River northwest of Portsmouth, New Hampshire, then into the Gulf of Maine.
response.summary_1: Berwick Academy is located in South Berwick and is described as "the oldest educational institution in Maine" and "sits ... on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire" (Passage 1). The Salmon Falls River "forms the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire" (Passage 2). Bridge entity / next-hop: confirm the state named in these passages (Maine) as the answer; use quoted anchors "oldest educational institution in Maine" and "overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire" to retrieve or verify the state's name.

Approximately 565 students in grades Pre-K through 12 (and Post-Grad) attend this coeducational day school, from approximately 60 communities in the surrounding regions of southern Maine, southeastern New Hampshire and northeastern Massachusetts.

Salmon Falls River | The Salmon Falls River is a tributary of the Piscataqua River in the U.S. states of Maine and New Hampshire.  It rises at Great East Lake, Newichawannock Canal, and Horn Pond and flows south-southeast for approximately 38 mi , forming the border between York County, Maine, and Strafford County, New Hampshire.

Horn Pond | Horn Pond is a 227 acre water body located on the border of Acton, Maine, and Wakefield, New Hampshire, in the United States.  The lake is both spring-fed and fed by the outflows of Great East Lake to the north and Wilson Lake to the east.  Horn Pond's outlet is the Salmon Falls River which forms a natural border between Maine and New Hampshire and empties into the Piscataqua River east of Dover, New Hampshire, and ultimately the Gulf of Maine.

Portland & Rochester Railroad | The Portland and Rochester Railroad, established in 1867, was an important predecessor railway of the Rochester to Portland branch line of the Boston and Maine Railroad.  It was founded in the merger of several smaller shortline rail transport companies, the oldest being the shortline York and Cumberland Railroad which was formed in 1846 to connect the seaport facilities of Portland, Maine, to the water powered manufacturing and textile industries along the Quampheagan Falls on the Salmon Falls River in the twin towns of South Berwick, Maine, and Rollinsford, New Hampshire (ca 1850).

Milton Pond | Milton Pond is a 271 acre water body located along the border between Strafford County, New Hampshire, and York County, Maine in the northeastern United States.  The lake lies in the towns of Milton, New Hampshire, and Lebanon, Maine.  It connects with Northeast Pond to the northeast, and with Town House Pond to the north.  A dam at the outlet of Milton Pond controls the water level for all three lakes.  Below the dam, the Salmon Falls River flows southeast along the Maine-New Hampshire border until it reaches the Piscataqua River.

Lovell Lake | Lovell Lake is a 538 acre water body in Carroll County in eastern New Hampshire, United States, in the town of Wakefield.  The lake is both spring-fed and fed by small streams including Horse Brook.  The outlet of the lake, located at the village of Sanbornville, is the beginning of the Branch River.  From here, the Branch River flows through Milton where it joins the Salmon Falls River on the Maine-New Hampshire border.  The Salmon Falls River empties into the tidal waters of the Piscataqua River northwest of Portsmouth, New Hampshire, then into the Gulf of Maine.

Vaughan Woods State Park | Vaughan Woods State Park is a public recreation area located along the Salmon Falls River on the western edge of South Berwick, Maine, at the New Hampshire border.  The state park includes the restored Hamilton House, stands of old-growth forest, and 3 mi of hiking trails.  The lands were bequeathed to the state by Elizabeth Vaughan in 1949.
response.summary_2: The state is Maine. Berwick Academy is described as "the oldest educational institution in Maine" and "sits ... on a hill overlooking the Salmon Falls River, near the border between Maine and New Hampshire," and the Salmon Falls River forms the border between York County, Maine and Strafford County, New Hampshire.
